content: Hyperkalemia is serum potassium >5.0-5.5 mEq/L, a potentially life-threatening electrolyte abnormality. Causes: (1) Decreased renal excretion (most common) - acute/chronic kidney disease, hypoaldosteronism (type 4 RTA, Addison disease), medications (ACE inhibitors, ARBs, spironolactone, eplerenone, amiloride, triamterene, trimethoprim, NSAIDs, heparin, calcineurin inhibitors); (2) Transcellular shift - acidosis, insulin deficiency, beta-blockers, digoxin toxicity, tumor lysis syndrome, rhabdomyolysis, succinylcholine, hyperkalemic periodic paralysis; (3) Increased intake (rare alone) - supplements, salt substitutes, IV potassium, blood transfusions. Pseudohyperkalemia from hemolysis during phlebotomy, thrombocytosis, leukocytosis. Clinical manifestations primarily cardiac: peaked T waves, PR prolongation, QRS widening, sine wave pattern, ventricular fibrillation/asystole. Neuromuscular: weakness, flaccid paralysis, paresthesias.
